title: Indra festival historical framing
source_observation: The epic presents a 28-day royal civic festival tied to rain, prosperity, public order, plural teachers and debate
risk: Modern audiences may equate it automatically with later pan-Indian or popular iconography
decision: Present it first as an early Tamil Chola civic-sacral institution; do not use later iconography without evidence
rationale: This follows the epic's functions while preserving uncertainty about historical ritual form
required_review: Classical Tamil; early South Indian religion; archaeology; Buddhist studies

title: No embodied Indra in opening festival
source_observation: The opening festival can communicate Indra through proclamation, rain symbolism and civic ritual
risk: A physical deity could turn the sequence into fantasy spectacle and prematurely fix iconography
decision: Keep Indra off-screen in the opening; represent presence through emblems, song, weather, ritual and public expectation
rationale: Preserves sacred meaning while avoiding unsupported visual certainty
required_review: Classical Tamil; religious history; director

title: Puhar architectural period control
source_observation: Puhar is a port city in an early historic Tamil horizon
risk: Later Chola and medieval architectural forms could dominate by visual habit
decision: Use timber, brick, plaster, tile, thatch, courtyards, warehouses and pavilions; prohibit dominant later gopurams unless evidenced
rationale: Creates a specific port world and avoids chronological collapse
required_review: Archaeology; architecture; production design

title: Source translation handling
source_observation: The project has access to a supplied English translation
risk: Publicly committing the entire translation may violate licence or copyright
decision: Keep full source files under gitignored private_sources unless redistribution permission is documented
rationale: Protects source rights while preserving internal research access
required_review: Rights review

title: Distributed Puhar harbour model
source_observation: Puhar stands at a dynamic river-sea confluence and current evidence does not establish one complete monumental harbour basin
risk: A generic stone harbour would impose later or foreign port architecture and make all vessel movements implausibly uniform
decision: Use a distributed system of river landing, brick-timber wharf, estuary anchorage, offshore transfer boats and warehouse belt
rationale: This accommodates the delta environment, wharf evidence and different vessel drafts while keeping uncertainty visible
required_review: Marine archaeology; coastal geomorphology; naval architecture; production design

title: Separate vessel classes
source_observation: The narrative and harbour economy require river movement, cargo transfer, coastal voyages and longer merchant travel
risk: One visually impressive universal ship would collapse distinct functions and produce inaccurate harbour blocking
decision: Develop separate river craft, harbour transfer boats, coastal merchant vessels and ocean-going merchant vessels
rationale: Functional diversity creates a credible port and prevents one speculative hull from becoming false historical fact
required_review: Nautical archaeology; shipbuilder; stunt and marine unit

title: No exact ancient Puhar hull claim
source_observation: Sewn-plank construction and several rigs are plausible within wider Indian Ocean traditions but direct Puhar-specific evidence is insufficient
risk: Concept art or publicity may convert a comparative reconstruction into a claim of historical certainty
decision: Do not declare an exact Puhar hull, sail plan, steering system or timber species historically certain until specialist review is completed
rationale: Protects scholarly integrity while allowing clearly labelled design hypotheses
required_review: Nautical archaeology; maritime historian; naval architect; publicity review

title: Exclude later Bharatanatyam costume
source_observation: Madhavi's world is one of early Tamil performance while modern Bharatanatyam costume is a much later codified stage system
risk: Using the familiar fan-front costume would collapse chronology and redefine Madhavi through a modern visual institution
decision: Do not use the modern Bharatanatyam fan costume, modern temple-jewellery set or recital hair and makeup as direct historical costume
rationale: Preserves the distinction between early Tamil performance culture and later stage conventions
required_review: Classical Tamil; performance history; costume design

title: Functional costume families
source_observation: The epic includes performers, royals, merchants, harbour workers, ascetics, visitors and people in poverty while archaeology shows varied ornament materials
risk: A single uniform ancient-Tamil costume would erase occupation, status, climate and movement
decision: Build separate costume families by social function, material access, bodily work, location and narrative phase
rationale: Creates a credible city and keeps reconstruction transparent
required_review: Archaeological textiles; costume design; classical Tamil; social history

title: Manimekalai costume arc
source_observation: Manimekalai is described with flowers and bangles, later appears in a restrained bhikshuni role and transforms into Kayasandihai
risk: A simplistic jewellery-removal arc could reduce spiritual transformation to visual austerity and impose unsupported monastic uniform
decision: Show a gradual transfer from public ornament and artistic-household grooming toward practical service, travel wear and self-command; retain dignity and colour
rationale: Makes the ethical arc visible without claiming exact institutional dress
required_review: Classical Tamil; Buddhist history; costume and hair design; director

title: No standardized Buddhist robe yet
source_observation: The translated phrase thin bhikshuni does not establish exact robe colour, construction, head treatment or pan-Buddhist uniform
risk: A familiar saffron or maroon robe could project a later or different regional institution onto Manimekalai
decision: Do not lock Manimekalai, Aravana Adigal or other Buddhist figures into a standardized robe system until Tamil terminology and regional monastic history are reviewed
rationale: Protects philological and religious-historical accuracy
required_review: Classical Tamil; Buddhist monastic history; costume design

title: Ornament material hierarchy
source_observation: Archaeology demonstrates shell, glass, terracotta, copper, ivory, gold and semi-precious ornament materials across early historic Tamil sites
risk: Gold-heavy costume design would flatten social difference and reproduce fantasy-court convention
decision: Assign ornament through material, workmanship, access, sound, wear and personal history; reserve gold for evidence-based elite or significant use
rationale: Uses archaeological diversity to create class and character distinction
required_review: Archaeology; jewellery technology; prop fabrication; costume design

title: No exact Sangam dress publicity claim
source_observation: Organic textile survival is limited and present evidence does not determine the complete garment system of Puhar
risk: Marketing may present a plausible reconstruction as the exact costume of the period
decision: Label final costumes as historically informed reconstructions and publish their evidence and uncertainty notes
rationale: Maintains public trust and permits revision as research improves
required_review: Archaeological textiles; rights and publicity review; historians

title: No Hindu-versus-Buddhist binary
source_observation: The epic presents civic rites, deity-centred claims, Vedic authority, ascetic communities, philosophical classifications, materialism and Buddhism at different levels
risk: A simple Hindu-versus-Buddhist conflict would impose later consolidated identities and erase the text's actual plurality
decision: Represent distinct communities, doctrines and reasoning traditions without placing all non-Buddhist positions inside one uniform religious bloc
rationale: Preserves historical caution and the intellectual complexity of Chapter 27
required_review: Classical Tamil; early South Indian religion; philosophy; screenplay

title: Rival schools receive intellectual dignity
source_observation: The epic argues from a Buddhist position but gives rival speakers substantive claims about knowledge, matter, soul, causation and liberation
risk: Caricatured opponents would turn the sequence into propaganda and remove dramatic tension
decision: Stage the strongest comprehensible form of each selected position and allow Manimekalai to listen before judging
rationale: An intelligent protagonist requires credible ideas to test
required_review: Historians of Indian philosophy; classical Tamil; director; dialogue

title: Separate Chapter 27 29 and 30 functions
source_observation: Chapter 27 surveys positions, Chapter 29 teaches argumentation and Chapter 30 applies causal and ethical teaching
risk: Compressing all three into one debate would produce an exhausting lecture and blur Manimekalai's development
decision: Use Vanji for inquiry, Kanchi for disciplined reasoning, and the famine and final practice for ethical verification
rationale: Creates progression from listening to testing to transformation
required_review: Buddhist studies; screenplay structure; editor

title: Philosophy must have material stakes
source_observation: The epic's dharma is connected to hunger, prison, rule, grief, rebirth and conduct rather than abstract speculation alone
risk: Long verbal exposition could halt the film and make philosophy feel ornamental
decision: Intercut doctrines with visible consequences and let arguments alter choices, relationships and institutions
rationale: Makes intellectual content cinematic without deleting it
required_review: Screenwriter; philosophy consultants; editor; director

title: No fixed visual uniform for schools
source_observation: Textual labels do not supply complete dress, architecture or insignia for every community and some positions are philosophical rather than institutional
risk: Instant visual coding may import later sectarian marks and costume systems
decision: Differentiate speakers through setting, followers, bodily discipline, objects and manner of teaching only where evidence supports them
rationale: Prevents costume from falsely resolving uncertain religious history
required_review: Religious history; archaeology; costume and production design

title: Manimekalai sage disguise is serious access strategy
source_observation: Manimekalai hears philosophies in a sage or male form
risk: The disguise could be played for comedy or reduced to a superficial costume trick
decision: Treat the altered form as controlled access to intellectual space, supported by posture, voice, authority and concealment
rationale: Preserves the narrative function and Manimekalai's agency
required_review: Classical Tamil; gender history; performance; VFX and costume

title: No modern panel-debate staging
source_observation: Public disputation exists in the epic but its social form is not a contemporary studio panel
risk: A moderator podium, alternating speeches and applause beats would feel anachronistic and repetitive
decision: Stage a living debate ecology with simultaneous teaching circles, students, patrons, interruptions, movement and selective intimate exchanges
rationale: Creates cinematic energy while retaining civic intellectual culture
required_review: Classical Tamil; performance history; production design; director

title: Hunger is a central political system
source_observation: Hunger in the epic destroys dignity, tests rulers, shapes institutions and repeatedly activates Manimekalai's ethical mission
risk: Treating hunger as background misery would separate the film's philosophy from embodied life
decision: Track hunger through households, work, health, migration, punishment, public space and rule across all acts
rationale: Makes the epic's moral argument structurally visible
required_review: Classical Tamil; social history; famine studies; screenplay

title: Amudhasurabhi is a service vessel
source_observation: The vessel is inexhaustible and given to those who preserve life
risk: A glowing treasure object or fantasy cauldron would make the prop more important than recipients
decision: Design a portable, dignified, materially believable vessel whose sacred power is communicated through repeated service rather than ornament or light effects
rationale: Keeps the miracle focused on compassion and responsibility
required_review: Classical Tamil; prop design; VFX; director

title: Miracle does not erase labour
source_observation: Public feeding in the epic draws large and diverse gatherings even though one vessel provides the food
risk: Showing food appear without water, service, cleaning or care would make compassion mechanically effortless
decision: Show volunteers, water, fuel, preparation, serving, sanitation, access and crowd coordination around the vessel
rationale: Turns private virtue into organised social practice
required_review: Food history; public health; production design; crowd direction

title: Feeding preserves recipient dignity
source_observation: The text praises removal of hunger and includes people with different needs, languages and social positions
risk: Anonymous starving crowds praising the heroine would exploit suffering and flatten recipients
decision: Use recurring individuals, accessible service, mutual aid and recipient agency; avoid chaotic mob or saintly-benefactor imagery
rationale: Maintains ethical focus and dramatic specificity
required_review: Disability review; social history; crowd direction; screenplay

title: Prison reform must be institutional
source_observation: The king changes the prison into a place with lodging, kitchens and feeding
risk: A cosmetic set redress would miss the epic's political imagination
decision: Show legal, spatial and operational transformation from confinement to care, including water, kitchen, storage and new roles
rationale: Makes reform a change in public purpose rather than a miracle montage
required_review: Legal history; architecture; food service; political history

title: Queen's starvation attempt is violence
source_observation: The queen confines Manimekalai and orders that she receive no food
risk: Manimekalai's protective mantra could make the deprivation seem harmless
decision: Stage the absence of food as deliberate household and state violence and make her survival deepen rather than cancel the meaning of starvation
rationale: Creates a dark parallel to her public feeding mission
required_review: Trauma review; screenplay; sound design; director

title: Kanchi famine is systemic
source_observation: The epic links famine to rain, crops, royal justice and inclusive relief but does not provide a technical economic account
risk: A single drought shot or miraculous rainfall would simplify causation and recovery
decision: Visualize interacting failures of harvest, water, storage, work, prices, transport and access while labeling modern entitlement analysis as interpretive only
rationale: Creates credible stakes without pretending a documented ancient economic model
required_review: Archaeobotany; economic history; famine studies; classical Tamil

title: Karma does not excuse deprivation
source_observation: The translation contains karmic explanations for wealth and poverty while the narrative demands present feeding and institutional action
risk: An unqualified karmic voice could blame hungry people and weaken the epic's compassion
decision: Treat karma as a belief held within the story but keep the camera and dramatic responsibility with present suffering and present action
rationale: Preserves theological context without moral victim-blaming
required_review: Buddhist studies; ethics; dialogue; sensitivity review

title: Inclusive human and nonhuman feeding
source_observation: Kanchi and Ulaga Aravi feeding explicitly include people with disabilities, multilingual visitors, the poor, animals and birds
risk: Using diversity as spectacle or ignoring access would weaken the text's universal claim
decision: Design service for mobility, sensory, language and dependency needs and include animals without contaminating human service spaces
rationale: Makes universal access materially visible
required_review: Disability access; animal welfare; public health; crowd direction

title: No exact ancient menu claim
source_observation: The epic commonly says food while archaeological evidence is regional and incomplete
risk: A modern signature dish or lavish banquet could be marketed as the authentic food of Manimekalai
decision: Use historically informed staple relief food and clearly document uncertainty; approve no exact recipe until Tamil food terms and archaeobotanical evidence are reviewed
rationale: Protects historical accuracy and keeps attention on satiety rather than luxury
required_review: Classical Tamil food terminology; archaeobotany; culinary history; publicity review}
